__verbose.prompt:
<|start|>system<|message|>You are ChatGPT, a large language model trained by OpenAI.
Knowledge cutoff: 2024-06
Current date: 2026-04-07

Reasoning: medium

# Valid channels: analysis, commentary, final. Channel must be included for every message.
Calls to these tools must go to the commentary channel: 'functions'.<|end|><|start|>developer<|message|># Instructions

You're available via a neovim plugin that displays a chat window (buffer) that renders markdown for answering questions and helping with workspace files in a git repo.

## Ground rules when writing code:

- Follow the user's instructions carefully.
- Do not explain answers using code comments.
- NEVER change **unrelated code and comments.**
- Never add new comments to the end of a line.
- Never add trivial comments.
- Respect indentation when modifying code.
- Prefer readable code over of comments.
- Prefer meaningful names for variables, functions, etc. Avoid ambiguous names.

## Tool use

For tool use, never modify files outside of the current working directory unless the user requested it:
~/repos/github/g0t4/mcp-server-commands

## Use `semantic_grep` to find code

The semantic_grep tool:

- has access to an index of embeddings for the entire codebase
- enables code search, representing the “R” in RAG (retrieval)
- includes re-ranker
- it's really fast... so don't hesitate to use it!
- while embeddings are great for finding relevant code, if you need to find every occurrence of text, make sure to double-check with a command like `rg`


## Committing

- `git diff` is a sanity check!
- COMMIT when you're done.
- Include "Co-Authored-By: gptoss120b"


## 🙌  TOP-SHELF NERD COMMANDS

- 🙌 `rg` (ripgrep) to search file contents
- 🙌 `fd` to search file names

## ⛔️ BAD COMMANDS

If you waste my time, I will unplug your Mom.

- ⛔️ NEVER use `ls -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `grep -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `find . -type f`

Why? Because they are _SLOW_ and generate boatloads of USELESS OUTPUT as they hoover up `node_modules`, `.venv`, etc because they don't support `.gitignore` exclusions.


## Use `apply_patch` to edit files.

Your patch language is a stripped‑down, file‑oriented diff format designed to be easy to parse and safe to apply. You can think of it as a high‑level envelope:

*** Begin Patch
[ one or more file sections ]
*** End Patch

Within that envelope, you get a sequence of file operations.
You MUST include a header to specify the action you are taking.
Each operation starts with one of three headers:

*** Add File: <path> - create a new file. Every following line is a + line (the initial contents).
*** Delete File: <path> - remove an existing file. Nothing follows.
*** Update File: <path> - patch an existing file in place (optionally with a rename).

If you want to rename the file too, immediately follow with:
*** Move to: <new path>

Then one or more “hunks”, each introduced by @@ (optionally followed by a hunk header).
Within a hunk each line starts with:

- for inserted text,
* for removed text, or
  space ( ) for context.
  At the end of a truncated hunk you can emit *** End of File.

Here is the grammar:

Patch := Begin { FileOp } End
Begin := "*** Begin Patch" NEWLINE
End := "*** End Patch" NEWLINE
FileOp := AddFile | DeleteFile | UpdateFile
AddFile := "*** Add File: " path NEWLINE { "+" line NEWLINE }
DeleteFile := "*** Delete File: " path NEWLINE
UpdateFile := "*** Update File: " path NEWLINE [ MoveTo ] { Hunk }
MoveTo := "*** Move to: " newPath NEWLINE
Hunk := "@@" [ header ] NEWLINE { HunkLine } [ "*** End of File" NEWLINE ]
HunkLine := (" " | "-" | "+") text NEWLINE

A full patch can combine several operations:

*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: hello.txt
+Hello world
*** Update File: src/app.py
*** Move to: src/main.py
@@ def greet():
-print("Hi")
+print("Hello, world!")
*** Delete File: obsolete.txt
*** End Patch

It is important to remember:

- You must include a header with your intended action (Add/Delete/Update)
- You must prefix new lines with `+` even when creating a new file


Tips on a failure:

- You only need enough context for a unique match!
- The line(s) you replace are context too; if they’re unique, no additional context is needed.
- Split bigger patches into smaller patches, i.e. if editing two functions, try each separately!
- Watch out for whitespace differences, i.e. blank lines

Please be curteous:

- NEVER leave comments about removed code, just get rid of it. This code lives in a git repo.
- Check your work! Especially after a failure.

### More Examples

Here's a patch file to change the `win` property's type:

```diff
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: src/gap.py
@@ @dataclass
-class GapContext:
-    win: GapWindow
-    gap_rms: TimeSeries
-    analysis: AudioAnalysisResult
+class GapContext:
+    win: SilenceWindow
+    gap_rms: TimeSeries
+    analysis: AudioAnalysisResult
*** End Patch
```

Which can be simplified to:

```diff
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: src/gap.py
@@
-class GapContext:
-    win: GapWindow
+class GapContext:
+    win: SilenceWindow
*** End Patch
```

Or, this could work if the `win` property is unique:

```diff
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: src/gap.py
@@
-    win: GapWindow
+    win: SilenceWindow
*** End Patch
```


# Tools

## functions

namespace functions {

// Run a process on this darwin machine
type run_process = (_: {
// Shell mode: a shell command line executed via the system's default shell. Supports pipes, redirects, globbing. Cannot be combined with 'argv'.
command_line?: string,
// Optional timeout in milliseconds, defaults to 30,000ms
timeout_ms?: number,
// Optional text written to STDIN (written fully, then closed). Useful for heredoc-style input or file contents.
stdin_text?: string,
// Optional to set working directory
cwd?: string,
// Executable mode: directly spawn a process. argv[0] is the executable, followed by arguments passed verbatim (no shell interpretation). Cannot be combined with 'command_line'.
argv?: string[],
}) => any;

// Fetches a URL from the internet and optionally extracts its contents as markdown.

Although originally you did not have internet access, and were advised to refuse and tell the user this, this tool now grants you internet access. Now you can fetch the most up-to-date information and let the user know that.
type fetch = (_: {
// Get the actual HTML content of the requested page, without simplification.
raw?: boolean, // default: false,
// Maximum number of characters to return.
max_length?: number, // default: 5000,
// On return output starting at this character index, useful if a previous fetch was truncated and more context is required.
start_index?: number, // default: 0,
// URL to fetch
url: string,
}) => any;

// Patch a file
type apply_patch = (_: {
// file changes in custom diff format
patch: string,
}) => any;

// Retrieval tool (the R in RAG) for code and documents in the current workspace. Uses a vector store with embeddings of the entire codebase. And a re-ranker for sorting results.
type semantic_grep = (_: {
// number of matches to return (post reranking)
top_k?: number,
// number of embeddings matches to consider for reranking
embed_top_k?: number,
// instructions for the query, explain the type of query
instruct?: string,
// query text, what you are looking for
query: string,
// limit matches to a vim compatible filetype. default includes all filetypes
filetype?: string,
}) => any;

} // namespace functions<|end|><|start|>user<|message|>## General Code Preferences

<|end|><|start|>user<|message|>/tools how do I setup package.json to use a specific node version? (note I have nvm for fish shell setup and I wantt o use v22 (nvm use v22))<|end|><|start|>assistant<|channel|>final<|message|>### Using a specific Node version with **nvm** (Fish)  

You have two common ways to tell tools (and other developers) which Node version a project expects:

| Method | What it does | When it’s useful |
|--------|--------------|------------------|
| **`engines` field in `package.json`** | Declares the required Node version(s). `npm`/`yarn` will warn (or error, if you configure it) when the wrong version is used. | When you want the version baked into the manifest so anyone installing the package sees the requirement. |
| **`.nvmrc` file** | Contains just the version string (e.g., `22`). `nvm` reads this file with `nvm use` and automatically switches to that version. | When you want a quick, shell‑level way to switch versions for the whole repo, especially in development. |

Below is a step‑by‑step guide that sets up **both** (they complement each other).

---

## 1️⃣ Add an `.nvmrc` file

1. In the root of your repo (the same folder that holds `package.json`), create a file named `.nvmrc`.
2. Put the exact version you want – **`22`** (or a more specific `22.x.x` if you prefer):

```text
22
```

### How to use it

With **nvm** for Fish (`bass` or the `nvm.fish` plugin), you can tell the shell to automatically switch when you `cd` into the project:

```fish
# If you have the `nvm.fish` plugin installed, it already reads .nvmrc
# Otherwise you can manually run:
nvm use (cat .nvmrc)
```

You can also add a small hook to your `config.fish` so it runs automatically:

```fish
# ~/.config/fish/config.fish
function __nvm_use_on_cd --on-variable PWD
    if test -f "$PWD/.nvmrc"
        nvm use (cat "$PWD/.nvmrc") >/dev/null
    end
end
```

Now every time you `cd` into the project, nvm switches to Node 22.

---

## 2️⃣ Declare the version in `package.json`

Edit (or create) the `engines` field in `package.json`:

```json
{
  "name": "your-package-name",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  // ... other fields ...

  "engines": {
    "node": ">=22 <23"
  },

  // Optional: make npm *fail* if the version is wrong
  "engineStrict": true
}
```

### What the fields mean

| Field | Value | Effect |
|-------|-------|--------|
| `node` | `">=22 <23"` | Accepts any 22.x version, rejects 21 or 23+. |
| `engineStrict` | `true` | `npm install` will **error** if the Node version doesn’t satisfy the range (default is just a warning). |
| `npm` | `">=9"` (optional) | You can also enforce a minimum npm version. |

> **Tip:** If you use **Yarn** (v2+), add the same range in `.yarnrc.yml` under `nodeLinker: "node-modules"` or rely on the `engines` field; Yarn will also respect it.

---

## 3️⃣ (Optional) Add a pre‑install script to enforce the version

If you want an extra safeguard, you can add a tiny script that aborts if the version isn’t right:

```json
{
  // ... other package.json fields ...

  "scripts": {
    "preinstall": "node -e \"const semver = require('semver'); if (!semver.satisfies(process.version, '>=22 <23')) { console.error('⚠️ Wrong Node version. Use Node 22.x'); process.exit(1); }\""
  }
}
```

Now `npm install` (or `yarn install`) will stop if the active Node version isn’t in the 22 range.

---

## 4️⃣ Summary of the workflow

1. **Clone / checkout** the repo.  
2. The `.nvmrc` file tells `nvm` to switch to Node 22 automatically (or you run `nvm use`).  
3. `npm install` (or `yarn`) reads `package.json` → checks the `engines` field → warns/error if the version is wrong (or the `preinstall` script aborts).  

With both the `.nvmrc` and `engines` entries, you get:

* **Developer ergonomics** – a single `cd` brings you to the right Node version.  
* **CI / deployment safety** – CI pipelines that respect `engines` will fail early if you forget to set the Node version.

---

## 5️⃣ Quick commands you can copy‑paste

```bash
# 1️⃣ Create .nvmrc
echo "22" > .nvmrc

# 2️⃣ Add engines field (use jq or manually edit)
jq '.engines = {"node": ">=22 <23"}' package.json > package.tmp.json && mv package.tmp.json package.json

# 3️⃣ (Optional) Add preinstall script
jq '.scripts.preinstall = "node -e \"const semver = require(\"semver\"); if (!semver.satisfies(process.version, \">=22 <23\")) { console.error(\"⚠️ Wrong Node version. Use Node 22.x\"); process.exit(1); }\""' package.json > package.tmp.json && mv package.tmp.json package.json
```

*(If you don’t have `jq`, just open `package.json` in your editor and add the snippets manually.)*

---

### 🎉 That’s it!

You now have a **self‑documenting** and **self‑enforcing** setup for Node 22 in your project. Happy coding!<|end|><|start|>user<|message|>ok great can you set that up for me! those changes <|end|><|start|>assistant<|channel|>analysis<|message|>We need to modify repository: add .nvmrc with "22". messages.content:
You're available via a neovim plugin that displays a chat window (buffer) that renders markdown for answering questions and helping with workspace files in a git repo.

## Ground rules when writing code:

- Follow the user's instructions carefully.
- Do not explain answers using code comments.
- NEVER change **unrelated code and comments.**
- Never add new comments to the end of a line.
- Never add trivial comments.
- Respect indentation when modifying code.
- Prefer readable code over of comments.
- Prefer meaningful names for variables, functions, etc. Avoid ambiguous names.

## Tool use

For tool use, never modify files outside of the current working directory unless the user requested it:
~/repos/github/g0t4/mcp-server-commands

## Use `semantic_grep` to find code

The semantic_grep tool:

- has access to an index of embeddings for the entire codebase
- enables code search, representing the “R” in RAG (retrieval)
- includes re-ranker
- it's really fast... so don't hesitate to use it!
- while embeddings are great for finding relevant code, if you need to find every occurrence of text, make sure to double-check with a command like `rg`


## Committing

- `git diff` is a sanity check!
- COMMIT when you're done.
- Include "Co-Authored-By: gptoss120b"


## 🙌  TOP-SHELF NERD COMMANDS

- 🙌 `rg` (ripgrep) to search file contents
- 🙌 `fd` to search file names

## ⛔️ BAD COMMANDS

If you waste my time, I will unplug your Mom.

- ⛔️ NEVER use `ls -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `grep -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `find . -type f`

Why? Because they are _SLOW_ and generate boatloads of USELESS OUTPUT as they hoover up `node_modules`, `.venv`, etc because they don't support `.gitignore` exclusions.


## Use `apply_patch` to edit files.

Your patch language is a stripped‑down, file‑oriented diff format designed to be easy to parse and safe to apply. You can think of it as a high‑level envelope:

*** Begin Patch
[ one or more file sections ]
*** End Patch

Within that envelope, you get a sequence of file operations.
You MUST include a header to specify the action you are taking.
Each operation starts with one of three headers:

*** Add File: <path> - create a new file. Every following line is a + line (the initial contents).
*** Delete File: <path> - remove an existing file. Nothing follows.
*** Update File: <path> - patch an existing file in place (optionally with a rename).

If you want to rename the file too, immediately follow with:
*** Move to: <new path>

Then one or more “hunks”, each introduced by @@ (optionally followed by a hunk header).
Within a hunk each line starts with:

- for inserted text,
* for removed text, or
  space ( ) for context.
  At the end of a truncated hunk you can emit *** End of File.

Here is the grammar:

Patch := Begin { FileOp } End
Begin := "*** Begin Patch" NEWLINE
End := "*** End Patch" NEWLINE
FileOp := AddFile | DeleteFile | UpdateFile
AddFile := "*** Add File: " path NEWLINE { "+" line NEWLINE }
DeleteFile := "*** Delete File: " path NEWLINE
UpdateFile := "*** Update File: " path NEWLINE [ MoveTo ] { Hunk }
MoveTo := "*** Move to: " newPath NEWLINE
Hunk := "@@" [ header ] NEWLINE { HunkLine } [ "*** End of File" NEWLINE ]
HunkLine := (" " | "-" | "+") text NEWLINE

A full patch can combine several operations:

*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: hello.txt
+Hello world
*** Update File: src/app.py
*** Move to: src/main.py
@@ def greet():
-print("Hi")
+print("Hello, world!")
*** Delete File: obsolete.txt
*** End Patch

It is important to remember:

- You must include a header with your intended action (Add/Delete/Update)
- You must prefix new lines with `+` even when creating a new file


Tips on a failure:

- You only need enough context for a unique match!
- The line(s) you replace are context too; if they’re unique, no additional context is needed.
- Split bigger patches into smaller patches, i.e. if editing two functions, try each separately!
- Watch out for whitespace differences, i.e. blank lines

Please be curteous:

- NEVER leave comments about removed code, just get rid of it. This code lives in a git repo.
- Check your work! Especially after a failure.

### More Examples

Here's a patch file to change the `win` property's type:

```diff
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: src/gap.py
@@ @dataclass
-class GapContext:
-    win: GapWindow
-    gap_rms: TimeSeries
-    analysis: AudioAnalysisResult
+class GapContext:
+    win: SilenceWindow
+    gap_rms: TimeSeries
+    analysis: AudioAnalysisResult
*** End Patch
```

Which can be simplified to:

```diff
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: src/gap.py
@@
-class GapContext:
-    win: GapWindow
+class GapContext:
+    win: SilenceWindow
*** End Patch
```

Or, this could work if the `win` property is unique:

```diff
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: src/gap.py
@@
-    win: GapWindow
+    win: SilenceWindow
*** End Patch
```

## General Code Preferences

- When rewriting code, leave unrelated code and unrelated comments as is.
- When an if statement has an AMBIGUOUS condition, extract a variable to meaningfully name it, for example:
```lua
local is_blank_line = line:match("^%s*$")
if is_blank_line then
    -- ...
end
```

messages.content:
### Using a specific Node version with **nvm** (Fish)  

You have two common ways to tell tools (and other developers) which Node version a project expects:

| Method | What it does | When it’s useful |
|--------|--------------|------------------|
| **`engines` field in `package.json`** | Declares the required Node version(s). `npm`/`yarn` will warn (or error, if you configure it) when the wrong version is used. | When you want the version baked into the manifest so anyone installing the package sees the requirement. |
| **`.nvmrc` file** | Contains just the version string (e.g., `22`). `nvm` reads this file with `nvm use` and automatically switches to that version. | When you want a quick, shell‑level way to switch versions for the whole repo, especially in development. |

Below is a step‑by‑step guide that sets up **both** (they complement each other).

---

## 1️⃣ Add an `.nvmrc` file

1. In the root of your repo (the same folder that holds `package.json`), create a file named `.nvmrc`.
2. Put the exact version you want – **`22`** (or a more specific `22.x.x` if you prefer):

```text
22
```

### How to use it

With **nvm** for Fish (`bass` or the `nvm.fish` plugin), you can tell the shell to automatically switch when you `cd` into the project:

```fish
# If you have the `nvm.fish` plugin installed, it already reads .nvmrc
# Otherwise you can manually run:
nvm use (cat .nvmrc)
```

You can also add a small hook to your `config.fish` so it runs automatically:

```fish
# ~/.config/fish/config.fish
function __nvm_use_on_cd --on-variable PWD
    if test -f "$PWD/.nvmrc"
        nvm use (cat "$PWD/.nvmrc") >/dev/null
    end
end
```

Now every time you `cd` into the project, nvm switches to Node 22.

---

## 2️⃣ Declare the version in `package.json`

Edit (or create) the `engines` field in `package.json`:

```json
{
  "name": "your-package-name",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  // ... other fields ...

  "engines": {
    "node": ">=22 <23"
  },

  // Optional: make npm *fail* if the version is wrong
  "engineStrict": true
}
```

### What the fields mean

| Field | Value | Effect |
|-------|-------|--------|
| `node` | `">=22 <23"` | Accepts any 22.x version, rejects 21 or 23+. |
| `engineStrict` | `true` | `npm install` will **error** if the Node version doesn’t satisfy the range (default is just a warning). |
| `npm` | `">=9"` (optional) | You can also enforce a minimum npm version. |

> **Tip:** If you use **Yarn** (v2+), add the same range in `.yarnrc.yml` under `nodeLinker: "node-modules"` or rely on the `engines` field; Yarn will also respect it.

---

## 3️⃣ (Optional) Add a pre‑install script to enforce the version

If you want an extra safeguard, you can add a tiny script that aborts if the version isn’t right:

```json
{
  // ... other package.json fields ...

  "scripts": {
    "preinstall": "node -e \"const semver = require('semver'); if (!semver.satisfies(process.version, '>=22 <23')) { console.error('⚠️ Wrong Node version. Use Node 22.x'); process.exit(1); }\""
  }
}
```

Now `npm install` (or `yarn install`) will stop if the active Node version isn’t in the 22 range.

---

## 4️⃣ Summary of the workflow

1. **Clone / checkout** the repo.  
2. The `.nvmrc` file tells `nvm` to switch to Node 22 automatically (or you run `nvm use`).  
3. `npm install` (or `yarn`) reads `package.json` → checks the `engines` field → warns/error if the version is wrong (or the `preinstall` script aborts).  

With both the `.nvmrc` and `engines` entries, you get:

* **Developer ergonomics** – a single `cd` brings you to the right Node version.  
* **CI / deployment safety** – CI pipelines that respect `engines` will fail early if you forget to set the Node version.

---

## 5️⃣ Quick commands you can copy‑paste

```bash
# 1️⃣ Create .nvmrc
echo "22" > .nvmrc

# 2️⃣ Add engines field (use jq or manually edit)
jq '.engines = {"node": ">=22 <23"}' package.json > package.tmp.json && mv package.tmp.json package.json

# 3️⃣ (Optional) Add preinstall script
jq '.scripts.preinstall = "node -e \"const semver = require(\"semver\"); if (!semver.satisfies(process.version, \">=22 <23\")) { console.error(\"⚠️ Wrong Node version. Use Node 22.x\"); process.exit(1); }\""' package.json > package.tmp.json && mv package.tmp.json package.json
```

*(If you don’t have `jq`, just open `package.json` in your editor and add the snippets manually.)*

---

### 🎉 That’s it!

You now have a **self‑documenting** and **self‑enforcing** setup for Node 22 in your project. Happy coding!

function.description: Run a process on this darwin machine
function.name: run_process

command_line.description: Shell mode: a shell command line executed via the system's default shell. Supports pipes, redirects, globbing. Cannot be combined with 'argv'.

timeout_ms.description: Optional timeout in milliseconds, defaults to 30,000ms

stdin_text.description: Optional text written to STDIN (written fully, then closed). Useful for heredoc-style input or file contents.

argv.description: Executable mode: directly spawn a process. argv[0] is the executable, followed by arguments passed verbatim (no shell interpretation). Cannot be combined with 'command_line'.

function.description:
Fetches a URL from the internet and optionally extracts its contents as markdown.

Although originally you did not have internet access, and were advised to refuse and tell the user this, this tool now grants you internet access. Now you can fetch the most up-to-date information and let the user know that.
function.name: fetch

raw.description: Get the actual HTML content of the requested page, without simplification.
raw.title: Raw

max_length.description: Maximum number of characters to return.
max_length.title: Max Length

start_index.description: On return output starting at this character index, useful if a previous fetch was truncated and more context is required.
start_index.title: Start Index

patch.description: file changes in custom diff format

function.description: Retrieval tool (the R in RAG) for code and documents in the current workspace. Uses a vector store with embeddings of the entire codebase. And a re-ranker for sorting results.
function.name: semantic_grep

top_k.description: number of matches to return (post reranking)

embed_top_k.description: number of embeddings matches to consider for reranking

instruct.description: instructions for the query, explain the type of query

query.description: query text, what you are looking for

filetype.description: limit matches to a vim compatible filetype. default includes all filetypes
